mnemonics.title: Usability Goals Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: Use the acronym 'SEE U LM' - S(afety), E(fficiency), E(ffectiveness), U(tility), L(earnability), M(emoriability)

mnemonics.title: Interaction Styles Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: Remember 'COMD' - C(Command), O(Objects), M(Menus), D(Direct Manipulation) for the four styles

mnemonics.title: Nielsen's Heuristics Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: Use 'PLACE UTC' - P(visibility), L(control), A(matches), C(consistency), E(error), U(help), T(recognize), C(feedback)

mnemonics.title: Fitts Law Key Point
mnemonics.content: BIG targets close = QUICK click; Small far = SLOW click - Remember 'Fast-Click-BIG-NEAR'

acronyms.usage: The multidisciplinary field of designing interactive computing systems
Visual-based interaction using graphics rather than text
Overall user perception and emotion during system interaction
Design methodology focusing on user needs throughout development
Cognitive model analyzing user task performance

acronyms.expansion: What You See Is What You Get
acronyms.usage: Interface showing exact output during editing

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a tennis racket (large, close) vs. a ping-pong paddle (small, far) - which is easier to hit? The large, close target like pressing a big button near cursor is easier.
Think of restaurant menus - a 2-page menu takes longer to decide than a 1-page menu with fewer items. More choices = longer decision time.
Star constellations - stars close together appear as a group even if unrelated. UI elements spaced closely are perceived as related.
Think of physically moving objects - dragging a file to trash feels like the real action. Visual feedback shows immediate results.
A door handle says 'pull', a push plate says 'push' - the object suggests its use. A button 'looks pressable'.

analogies.analogy: Mental model is like a map in your head - just as you navigate a city using your mental map, users navigate software using their internal understanding.
Feedback is like a conversation - when you speak, you expect a response. Without feedback, interaction feels like talking to a wall.
Affordances are like physical cues - a teacup's handle says 'hold me here', a chair's armrest says 'rest here' - digital objects should similarly suggest their use.
Visibility in UI is like dashboard indicators in a car - you need to see speed, fuel, temperature to drive safely.
Consistency is like traffic rules - once you learn 'red means stop', it applies everywhere. Consistent interfaces reduce learning for each new application.
